query: pathology of enameloma and its relation to ameloblast differentiation

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ameloblasts are of ectodermal origin and derived from oral epithelium. The cells are only present during tooth development that deposit tooth enamel, which forms the outer surface of the crown. Ameloblasts become functional only after odontoblasts form the primary layer of dentin (the layer beneath enamel). The cells eventually become part of the enamel epithelium and eventually undergo apoptosis (cell death) before or after tooth eruption.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Ameloblastic fibroma may share the same features histologically, such as small cords and islands of ameloblastic epithelium that's just two cells thick containing dense collagenous stroma which is often immature. It can also occasionally contain cementum or dentin production as well as stellate reticulum. The main feature of ameloblastic fibroma is that the stroma is more primitive and it should not metastasize.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise Only From Odontogenic Epithelium Without Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme
retrieved_docs.content: A calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor (Pindborg tumor) also arises mainly from odontogenic epithelium. It usually originates from the remnants of the dental lamina or reduced enamel epithelium. Histologically, it appears as sheets of polygonal cells surrounded by fibrous stroma with areas of eosinophilic amyloid-like materials. Calcification is a distinctive feature and usually develops within the amyloid-like material and forms rings, known as Liesegang’s rings. The exact nature of the eosinophilic material is controversial as it does not stain as amyloid when exposed to Congo red. However, it exhibits apple-green birefringence when seen by polarized light. This material may represent enamel protein that is secreted by the tumor cells. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Pathophysiology -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise Only From Odontogenic Epithelium Without Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme
retrieved_docs.content: Other types in this category include calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumors (Pindborg tumor), which usually arises from the remnants of the dental lamina or reduced enamel epithelium, and squamous odontogenic tumors that usually originate from the remnants of the dental lamina. [16] The adenomatoid odontogenic tumor usually arises from odontogenic epithelium around the crowns of unerupted teeth. It is considered by many as a hamartoma. It was previously considered as a variant of ameloblastoma named adenoameloblastoma. However, a better understanding of the biological behavior of this tumor changed this concept. It is biologically non-aggressive. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histologically, it is most important to differentiate common ameloblastomas vs. ameloblastic carcinoma/malignant ameloblastoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There exist deposits of these cells in the structures in and around the tooth, termed cell rests of Malessez and cell rests of Serres. Current thought is that ameloblastomas can arise from either the cells mentioned above or other cells of ectodermal origin, such as those associated with the enamel organ. Approximately 80% occur in the mandible and the other 20% in the maxilla.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic tumors that arise from ectomesenchyme with or without included odontogenic epithelium:
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis is challenging in small biopsies as it can mimic other head and neck tumors. Finally, odontogenic sarcoma and carcinosarcoma in which epithelial component is similar to ameloblastoma, or ameloblastic fibroma. However, the mesenchymal component shows malignant features. With recurrence, the stromal component increases, and the epithelial component diminishes. If dysplastic dentin is present in ameloblastic fibrosarcoma, then it is called ameloblastic fibrodentinosarcoma. While if it additionally shows foci of dysplastic enamel protein, it is called ameloblastic fibro-odontosarcoma. Moreover, if both carcinomatous and malignant spindle cell components are present, it is then odontogenic carcinosarcoma. [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Enamel formation (Amelogenesis): Enamel formation starts immediately after the first layer of dentin is laid down by the odontoblasts. The cells from the inner enamel epithelium differentiate into ameloblasts. These are columnar cells attached to the stratum intermedium via ita base. A pyramidal extension is present at the secretory end of ameloblasts known as Tomes' process through which the enamel matrix is secreted at the amelodentinal junction. Once the enamel matrix is laid down, immediately, the mineralization process begins by secretion of calcium and phosphate ions into the enamel matrix, forming hydroxyapatite crystallites. As more matrixes are secreted and mineralized, the ameloblast cells move away from the amelodentinal junction forming a pattern of crystallites contained within enamel prisms. These prisms are also known as enamel rods as they run from the amelodentinal junction to the enamel surface.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Pathophysiology -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise Only From Odontogenic Epithelium Without Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme
retrieved_docs.content: They include ameloblastoma, calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor, squamous odontogenic tumor, and adenomatoid odontogenic tumor. Ameloblastoma is the second most common odontogenic tumor after odontoma. It can originate from any of the following: remnants of the dental lamina, enamel organ, odontogenic cyst lining, or oral mucosa basal cells. It can be either central (intra-osseous), which includes multicystic or solid and unicystic or peripheral (extra-osseous). The majority of cases are solid or multicystic 92%, while unicystic and peripheral represent 6% and 2%, respectively. [14] Recurrent and aggressive ameloblastoma are associated with a high number of PCNA-positive cells, overexpression of BCL2, BCLX, IL1, and IL6. [15] Malignant ameloblastoma has been removed from the malignant category and added to the ameloblastoma group in the most recent edition of the WHO classification due to its bland histological features.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cause of ameloblastoma is not well understood. There is recent evidence that genetic mutations that activate a specific signaling pathway (MAPK) play a role in the pathogenesis of ameloblastoma. Further understanding of the molecular basis of tumorigenesis will have implications for diagnosis and therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise Only From Odontogenic Epithelium Without Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme
retrieved_docs.content: Multi-cystic or solid type: It usually originates de novo, and it has many variants, including follicular subtype in which follicles of epithelial cells are present in connective tissue stroma. It is composed of loosely arranged stellate cells in the center and columnar cells in the periphery. These follicles are like the enamel organ seen in the early stages of tooth development. Cystic changes may be seen as well. Plexiform subtype: arranged in long anastomosing sheets of odontogenic epithelium. It may lack reverse polarization and may not resemble any stages of tooth development. It is called a “fishnet pattern.” Cystic changes are uncommon.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The word ameloblastoma derives from the early English word “amel,” meaning enamel and the Greek word “blastos,” meaning germ. They are rare, odontogenic tumors, thought to be composed of the epithelium of ectodermal origin, which means they are tumors arising from the cells around the tooth root, or in close approximation, derived from the ectoderm germ layer. Ameloblastomas represent about 1% of all jaw tumors, but they are the second-most common odontogenic tumor. They are much more common in the lower jaw than in the upper jaw, and more common in the posterior mandible as compared to the anterior. The vast majority of the time, they are a benign tumor with aggressive behavior; however, rarely they can develop into, or be associated with, a malignancy (malignant ameloblastoma or ameloblastic carcinoma). It is extremely rare to find ameloblastomas outside the maxilla and mandible due to the association with teeth and their structures.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: It is difficult to differentiate benign ameloblastoma from malignant ameloblastoma on histology alone as it can look benign on histology but be clinically invasive or even metastasize. Benign ameloblastoma is histologically identical to malignant ameloblastoma but has no metastases. However, malignant variations are extremely rare, with only approximately 30 cases of malignant ameloblastoma in the literature, and about several hundred cases of ameloblastic carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The enamel organ comprises 4 layers: the inner and outer enamel epithelium, the stratum intermedium, and the stellate reticulum. The enamel is made before a tooth erupts into the mouth by the enamel organ, which contains cells called ameloblasts. These cells die once the tooth erupts, which means that the ability to make enamel is forever lost once eruption occurs. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: As the enamel matures, the crystallites within the enamel increase in size, and the organic content reduces. Once the enamel formation completes, the ameloblast cell loses the Tomes' process, flattens, and becomes the reduced enamel epithelium. This layer protects the enamel during eruption and eventually become the junctional epithelium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72

This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.
